source.title: Hippocampal Contributions to Model-Based Planning and Spatial Memory

confidence_rationale: Human lesion study (anterior temporal lobectomy with hippocampal resection) demonstrating that hippocampal damage impairs model-based planning in a two-step decision task, with impairment magnitude correlating with right hippocampal lesion size. Provides direct human causal evidence for the hippocampal-planning system in MECH-163. Coauthors include Daw (the computational RL theorist behind the model-free/model-based dissociation framework), increasing the rigor of behavioral classification. Confidence reduced slightly by unilateral lesion design and confound of overall temporal lobe damage.
failure_signatures: model-based planning impaired in hippocampal resection patients
impairment correlates with right hippocampal lesion size
planning and spatial memory covary in controls but not patients

mapping.source_claim_statement: Hippocampal resection in epilepsy patients impairs both model-based decision-making (two-step task) and spatial place memory. The model-based planning impairment scales with right hippocampal lesion size, and planning and place memory covary in healthy controls but this relationship is attenuated in patients -- suggesting shared hippocampal substrate for both functions.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-163 requires that the hippocampally-planned system be necessary (not just sufficient) for model-based behavior. Vikbladh et al. provide human causal evidence that hippocampal damage impairs model-based planning in direct proportion to lesion magnitude. This bridges the rodent evidence (Miller et al. 2017) to human behavior and directly supports MECH-163's claim that the hippocampal system is required -- not optional -- for model-based goal-directed planning. The covariation between planning and spatial memory in controls (but not patients) supports the REE claim that hippocampal mapping underpins long-horizon benefit accumulation.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Two-step task is a laboratory proxy for model-based planning; it tests contingency learning over dozens of trials, not prospective long-horizon planning per se. Does not test prosocial planning. Unilateral lesions limit causal inference strength. The 'novel context' component of MECH-163 is not explicitly tested -- patients use a familiar task.
mapping.source_context: Case-control study: healthy controls vs. anterior temporal lobectomy patients with hippocampal resection. NYU, Columbia, UCL collaboration. Nathaniel Daw as senior author provides strong computational RL framing for model-based classification. Neuron 2019.

confidence_components.notes: Human study with direct translational value (no cross-species transfer risk). Neuron publication with strong methodological team. Mapping fidelity high for hippocampal necessity claim, moderate for novel-context and long-horizon aspects. The two-step task operationalizes model-based planning well but is not isomorphic to long-horizon prosocial planning in MECH-163.
